title: Wrist arthroscopy portals and TFCC anatomy (radiocarpal and midcarpal)
summary: Standard radiocarpal and midcarpal portals with specific risks. TFCC components and central disc avascularity.
must_know: Position: supine, elbow flexed 90deg, 10lb finger traction tower. Landmarks: Lister's tubercle, scaphoid, lunate, DRUJ, ECU.

Radiocarpal portals (nick and spread after sharp skin inc): 3-4 (1cm distal Lister between EPL/EDC; primary viewing portal; risk EPL/EDC tendons), 4-5 (ring finger MC line between EDC/EDM; instrumentation/TFCC view; risk EDC/EDM), 6R (just radial to ECU; primary ulnar TFCC; risk dorsal sensory ulnar ~8mm), 6U (ulnar to ECU; ulnar TFCC; same risk), 1-2 (snuffbox between APL/ECRB; radial styloid access; risk superficial radial n + radial a).

Midcarpal portals (necessary for complete carpal visualization and instability): MCR (1cm distal 3-4 along radial middle MC between ECRB/EDC; views SL, SC, STT), MCU (1cm distal 4-5 ring MC; views LC, LT, TH).
TFCC: dorsal and volar radioulnar ligaments, central disc (avascular, poor healing potential), meniscus homolog, ulnocarpal ligaments.
Scope: 2.7mm 30deg most common. Distend joint first to protect structures.
portals: 6R/6U: ulnar TFCC, dorsal sensory ulnar ~8mm risk
1-2: radial, supf radial + radial a risk
structures_at_risk: Dorsal sensory branch of ulnar nerve (averages 8mm from 6R/6U portals; highest risk for TFCC work).
Superficial radial nerve and radial artery (1-2 portal in snuffbox).
procedure_specific_relevance: Systematic portal use and TFCC evaluation critical for ulnar-sided wrist pain, instability, and triangular fibrocartilage pathology. Complications 1-2%, mostly nerve.
common_pimp_questions: Which portal is primary for viewing the TFCC and what nerve is ~8mm away?
List the radiocarpal portals and the primary structure at risk for each.
Why are midcarpal portals required for complete evaluation of carpal instability?
What is the vascularity of the central TFCC disc and implications for repair vs debridement?
retrieval_phrases: dorsal sensory ulnar nerve 8mm wrist scope
notes: Created from source_library 6009 wrist-arthroscopy (success). Full portal list + TFCC + risks for diagnostic/therapeutic use.

title: Wrist arthroscopy systematic diagnostic sequence and TFCC pathology recognition
summary: From radial to ulnar, proximal to distal; recognize TFCC tears, SL/LT instability, chondral injury.
must_know: Start with 3-4 portal 30deg scope: systematic sweep radial to ulnar, proximal to distal.

Key structures to visualize and probe: radial styloid, scaphoid fossa, lunate fossa, TFCC (dorsal/volar RU ligs, central disc, meniscus homolog), SL interval (probe for Geissler grading), LT interval.
Midcarpal: assess SL (from MCR), LT (from MCU), STT, TH.
TFCC pathology: central disc tears (debride if avascular), peripheral (repair if vascular), foveal avulsion (DRUJ instability).
Document chondral lesions (ICRS or Outerbridge), synovitis, loose bodies.
diagnostic_sequence: 3-4 portal entry, distend, systematic radial to ulnar sweep.
Visualize and probe TFCC (traction test for foveal), SL (Geissler), LT.
Switch to 4-5 or 6R for ulnar details and instrumentation.
Midcarpal portals for interosseous and midcarpal joints.
Document all findings; correlate with preop imaging and exam.
systematic_checklist: Radial gutter -> TFCC -> SL -> LT -> midcarpal -> ulnar gutter. Probe every interval. Traction test TFCC fovea.
common_pimp_questions: What is the Geissler classification used for in wrist arthroscopy?
How do you differentiate a repairable peripheral TFCC tear from a central degenerative one?
Why start with the 3-4 portal and what is the first structure typically visualized?
retrieval_phrases: Geissler SL LT grading wrist scope
notes: Companion to portal module; from 6009 source. | Enriched v1_3 from source_library_v2

title: Elbow arthroscopy portals, landmarks, structures at risk, diagnostic sequence
summary: Standard portals with specific NV risks; systematic diagnostic from lateral to medial.
must_know: Portals (standard): proximal anterolateral (1-2cm prox, 1cm ant to lat epicondyle - radial n risk decreases proximally), distal anterolateral (1-3cm distal 1cm ant - PIN, lateral a
Landmarks: olecranon, medial and lateral epicondyles, radiocapitellar joint, ulnar nerve (palpate).
Technique: distend joint first (20-30ml) to displace NV structures; nick and spread technique for portals.
Risks: radial/PIN and lateral antebrachial cutaneous (anterolateral portals), ulnar (proximal anteromedial ~7mm), median (anteromedial).
structures_at_risk: Portals (standard): proximal anterolateral (1-2cm prox, 1cm ant to lat epicondyle - radial n risk decreases proximally), distal anterolateral (1-3cm distal 1cm ant - PIN, lateral a
Landmarks: olecranon, medial and lateral epicondyles, radiocapitellar joint, ulnar nerve (palpate).
Risks: radial/PIN and lateral antebrachial cutaneous (anterolateral portals), ulnar (proximal anteromedial ~7mm), median (anteromedial).
landmarks: Landmarks: olecranon, medial and lateral epicondyles, radiocapitellar joint, ulnar nerve (palpate).
diagnostic_sequence: Direct lateral entry for posterior view
common_pimp_questions: Which portal has the highest risk to the radial nerve/PIN?
Why distend the joint before portal placement?
What is the safest portal for initial distension and posterior viewing?
retrieval_phrases: direct lateral soft spot elbow scope
elbow scope radial PIN ulnar risk
notes: Created v1_3 from source_library_v2 elbow 3088 concrete facts.
